d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Phoenician fleet completes circumnavigation of Africa
v.s: Egyptian Pharaoh Necho II (r. 610–595 BCE) sponsored a fleet of Phoenician sailors who set out from the Red Sea and sailed south along Africa's east coast. Each autumn they went ashore to sow and harvest grain before continuing. After about three years, they returned to Egypt via the Strait of Gibraltar, completing the first recorded circumnavigation of Africa and proving that the continent is surrounded by sea.

v.t: King Zhuang of Chu marches north to inquire about the Nine Tripods, establishing Chu's hegemony
v.s: King Zhuang of Chu (Xiong Lü, r. 613–591 BCE) revitalized the state through internal reforms and military development, employing capable ministers such as Sun Shu'ao. In 606 BCE, under the pretext of attacking the Lu Hun barbarians, he led his army to the outskirts of the Eastern Zhou capital Luoyi, held a grand military review, and deliberately asked about the weight and size of the Zhou royal Nine Tripods, openly challenging the authority of the Son of Heaven. This act marked Chu's deep penetration into the Central Plains and its formal recognition as one of the Five Hegemons of the Spring and Autumn Period, profoundly altering the political landscape.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Milesian philosopher Anaximander proposes the 'apeiron' as the arche
v.s: Anaximander of Miletus (c. 610–546 BCE) argued that the arche (first principle) of all things is not a specific substance but an eternal, indestructible, and boundless entity called the 'apeiron' (the infinite). From this he explained the generation and destruction of opposing elements and drew the first known map of the world. His thought marked a leap in Greek philosophy from concrete substance to abstract principle.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Jehoiachin becomes king of Judah, facing Babylonian threat
v.s: After the death of Jehoiakim, his son Jehoiachin ascended the throne. At that time, the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadnezzar II had risen to become the hegemon of Western Asia. Jehoiachin attempted to resist Babylon with Egyptian help, but this risky policy heightened tensions with Babylon and directly led to the fall of Jerusalem the following year.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Babylon captures Jerusalem, exiles Jehoiachin
v.s: Because King Jehoiachin of Judah rebelled against Babylon after only three months on the throne, Nebuchadnezzar II laid siege to Jerusalem. Jehoiachin surrendered, and Nebuchadnezzar looted the Temple and palace treasures, exiling Jehoiachin, the royal family, nobles, and artisans—over 10,000 people—to Babylon, an event known as the 'First Deportation of Judah.' The Babylonians set Jehoiachin's uncle Zedekiah as puppet king, reducing Judah to a vassal state.

v.t: Battle of Bi: Chu defeats Jin, securing hegemony over the Central Plains
v.s: To vie for control of the Central Plains, King Zhuang of Chu personally led his army against the forces of Jin at Bi (northeast of present-day Xingyang, Henan). The Chu army exploited divisions and poor command within the Jin forces, using a surprise attack to rout them. The fleeing Jin troops crowded onto boats to cross the river, causing many casualties. After this battle, Chu replaced Jin as the chief hegemon of the Central Plains, and King Zhuang's status was formally recognized.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Ancient Greek Olympic Games add events for boys
v.s: To encourage youth participation in athletics, the Olympic Games officially added wrestling, running, and boxing events for boys. This reform made the Games more systematic and professional, attracting talented youths from across the Greek world and strengthening the religious and national cohesion of the Olympics.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: King Zhuang of Chu lays prolonged siege to Song, forcing its submission
v.s: Because the state of Song killed a Chu envoy, King Zhuang of Chu laid siege to its capital, Shangqiu. After nine months, the people of Song had exhausted food and supplies, even resorting to exchanging children for food. Eventually, Song was forced to negotiate a humiliating treaty recognizing Chu's hegemony. This event further consolidated Chu's prestige in the Central Plains.

v.t: Lyric poet Pindar of ancient Greece begins his creative work
v.s: The Theban poet Pindar (c. 522–443 BCE) would only reach his creative maturity in the early 5th century BCE—this entry is about 70 years too early. Pindar is known for his magnificent choral lyric poetry celebrating Olympic victors, profoundly influencing later poetry. This should have been a prelude to his birth, not his actual creative period.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Solon's reforms in Athens lay foundation for democracy
v.s: Facing severe wealth disparity and class conflict in Athens, the archon Solon (c. 638–558 BCE) enacted major reforms: the 'Seisachtheia' cancelling all debts and debt-slavery; dividing citizens into four property classes with political rights corresponding to wealth; and creating the Council of Four Hundred and the people's jury courts. Solon's reforms neither fully satisfied the commoners nor preserved aristocratic privilege, but they laid the groundwork for Athenian slave-owning democracy.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Chu Prime Minister Sun Shu'ao oversees water conservancy project
v.s: The Chu Prime Minister Sun Shu'ao (c. 630–593 BCE) oversaw the construction of one of China's earliest large-scale water conservancy projects—the Quebei Reservoir (present-day Anfengtang in Shou County, Anhui). By damming low-lying land to form a reservoir that could irrigate vast tracts of farmland, it greatly enhanced Chu's grain reserves and economic power, providing solid material support for King Zhuang's hegemonic ambitions.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Jin minister Xi Ke humiliated on mission to Qi, sowing seeds for war
v.s: Xi Ke, the chief minister of Jin, went on a diplomatic mission to Qi. At court, he was mocked for his limp by the mother of Duke Qing of Qi. Feeling deeply humiliated, Xi Ke returned to Jin and asked Duke Jing to attack Qi. Although permission was not granted, he channeled his anger into strengthening Jin's military, laying the direct cause for the future Battle of An between Jin and Qi.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Death of the great hegemon King Zhuang of Chu; Chu's hegemony falters
v.s: King Zhuang of Chu (Xiong Lü), one of the Five Hegemons of the Spring and Autumn Period, died and was succeeded by his son Shen (King Gong of Chu). During his reign, King Zhuang used Sun Shu'ao to reform domestic affairs and won the Battle of Bi, securing Chu's hegemony over the Central Plains. His death destabilized Chu's position, and Jin seized the opportunity to contend again for control of the Central Plains.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Hanging Gardens of Babylon at their zenith
v.s: According to tradition, the Neo-Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II built the magnificent 'Hanging Gardens' for his wife Amytis of Media, who missed the green hills of her homeland. The gardens used tiered platforms with exotic plants and a mechanical irrigation system that drew water from the Euphrates, making them appear to hang in the air. They were later celebrated as one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

v.t: Jin forms alliance with Wei and Zheng to counter Chu
v.s: After the death of King Zhuang of Chu, Duke Jing of Jin seized the opportunity to meet with Wei and Zheng at Suo (present-day Yuanyang, Henan) in 590 BCE, reforming the anti-Chu coalition. This marked the return of the decades-long tug-of-war between Jin and Chu, raising tensions in the Central Plains.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Battle of An: Jin defeats Qi, Qi sues for peace
v.s: To avenge Xi Ke's humiliation and counter Duke Qing of Qi's challenge to Jin's hegemony, Jin fielded 800 chariots in 589 BCE and engaged Qi forces at An (present-day Jinan, Shandong). Despite serious wounds, the Jin commander Xi Ke kept beating the war drum, routing the Qi army and forcing Duke Qing to send an envoy for peace, returning territory seized from Lu and Wei and acknowledging Jin's supremacy.

v.t: Birth of the ancient Greek mathematician Pythagoras
v.s: The famous ancient Greek mathematician and philosopher Pythagoras was born on the island of Samos around 570 BCE—about 19 years earlier than this entry. He later founded the Pythagorean school in southern Italy, proposed the philosophy that 'all is number', proved the Pythagorean theorem (the gougu theorem), and made epochal contributions to mathematics, music theory, and astronomy.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Greeks begin minting silver coins, spurring a commercial revolution
v.s: Building on coinage invented by the Lydian kingdom in Asia Minor, Greek poleis such as Aegina, Corinth, and Athens began minting their own silver coins on a large scale. The standardization and portability of coins greatly facilitated inter-polis trade, promoted the development of a commodity and monetary economy, and also supported state finances and mercenary armies.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Zedekiah rebels against Babylon; Jerusalem besieged again
v.s: Encouraged by Egypt, King Zedekiah of Judah declared independence from Babylon. An enraged Nebuchadnezzar II again led his army west and laid siege to Jerusalem. Although Zedekiah briefly received Egyptian reinforcements, the Egyptian army was defeated by Babylon, and Jerusalem endured a harrowing eighteen-month siege with severe famine.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Babylon captures Jerusalem, ending the Kingdom of Judah
v.s: Nebuchadnezzar II breached the walls of Jerusalem, and the Kingdom of Judah fell. Zedekiah tried to flee but was captured near Jericho; before his eyes his sons were killed, after which his own eyes were gouged out, and he was bound in bronze chains and taken to Babylon. The Babylonians completely destroyed Jerusalem and the First Temple, deporting most of the inhabitants—the 'Babylonian Captivity'.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Thales accurately predicts a solar eclipse, halting a war
v.s: Using Babylonian astronomical knowledge, the Greek philosopher Thales successfully predicted a solar eclipse on May 28, 585 BCE. The armies of Media and Lydia, locked in battle near the Halys River, were terrified when day suddenly turned to night, interpreting it as a divine sign and immediately calling a truce. The peace led to the end of a six-year war through intermarriage and territorial division.

v.t: Laozi serves as keeper of the Zhou royal archives
v.s: Around this time, the great ancient Chinese philosopher and founder of Daoism, Laozi (Li Er, courtesy name Dan), served as keeper of the Zhou royal archives (equivalent to a national librarian). This position gave him access to classical texts and records, allowing him to deeply comprehend the workings of ancient governance and the natural way of Heaven, laying the foundation for his later writing of the Tao Te Ching.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Jin moves its capital to Xintian, known as 'New Jiang'
v.s: To escape the grip of old aristocratic families at the old capital (Gu Jiang) and to strengthen control over the eastern vassals, Duke Jing of Jin ordered the capital moved from Jiang (present-day Yicheng, Shanxi) to Xintian (present-day Houma, Shanxi), later called 'New Jiang'. The new city was better planned, with rationally arranged palaces, ancestral temples, and craft workshops, showing Jin's determination to revive its hegemony after years of internal strife.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Duke Jing of Jin orders massacre of the Zhao clan, the 'Disaster of the Lower Palace'
v.s: Stirred up by the powerful minister Tu'an Gu, Duke Jing of Jin alleged that Zhao Dun had been complicit in the murder of Duke Ling of Jin and ordered the extermination of the Zhao clan—the 'Disaster of the Lower Palace'. The orphan Zhao Wu survived thanks to the sacrifices of the retainers Cheng Ying and Gongsun Chujiu. This event epitomized the brutal struggles among the ministers of Jin and planted the seeds for the later 'Partition of Jin into Three'.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Pythian Games officially founded at Delphi
v.s: Following the Olympics, the Greeks held the first Pythian Games at the sanctuary of Apollo in Delphi. Originally a musical and athletic competition celebrating Apollo's victory over the serpent Python, the winners received laurel wreaths. Along with the Olympics, Nemean, and Isthmian Games, the Pythian Games became one of the four Panhellenic festivals, strengthening cultural identity among Greek city-states.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: King Gong of Chu allies with Qin against Jin
v.s: Facing Jin's resurgence as hegemon, King Gong of Chu sent envoys to Qin, and the two states held a covenant in Qin territory, establishing a strategic alliance to resist Jin. This north-south coalition put great pressure on Jin's hegemony, leading to a complex structure of four great powers—Jin, Chu, Qin, and Qi—in the mid-to-late Spring and Autumn period.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Rulers of Jin and Qin meet at Linghu
v.s: To ease diplomatic pressure from the Qin-Chu alliance, Duke Li of Jin met with Duke Huan of Qin at Linghu (present-day Linyi, Shanxi) in an attempt to ease decades of hostility. However, Duke Huan broke the covenant after returning home and secretly joined Chu and the Di tribes in attacking Jin, soon shattering the brief peace.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: First Disarmament Conference: Jin and Chu briefly cease hostilities
v.s: Mediated by Hua Yuan, a minister of Song, Jin, Chu, and their allies held the first disarmament conference at Song's capital Shangqiu. They agreed not to attack each other and to jointly punish any vassal that violated the agreement. This was the first peace accord voluntarily reached by major powers in the Spring and Autumn period, but it lasted only three years before being broken by Chu.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Servius Tullius becomes king of Rome, initiates reforms
v.s: Servius Tullius became the sixth king of Rome. He carried out far-reaching military and political reforms: dividing citizens into five classes based on property and forming the comitia centuriata; dividing Roman territory into tribes to replace blood ties for taxation and conscription. These reforms greatly strengthened Rome's military and laid the foundation for the later Roman Republic.

v.t: Duke Li of Jin defeats Qin at Masui
v.s: To retaliate for Duke Huan of Qin breaking the covenant and allying with the Di to attack Jin, Duke Li of Jin joined with eight other states (including Qi, Song, Wei, and Zheng) to invade Qin. The decisive battle at Masui (present-day Jingyang, Shaanxi) resulted in a great defeat for Qin. This blow crippled Qin for decades, ensuring Jin's control over the western situation.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Zheng defects from Chu to Jin, causing southern turmoil
v.s: Unwilling to bear Chu's heavy taxes, Duke Cheng of Zheng, under Jin's persuasion, declared independence from Chu and allied with Jin. This infuriated King Gong of Chu, who immediately attacked Zheng. Zheng became a constant battleground between the two hegemons, suffering years of war that exhausted its people and treasury.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Greek philosopher Anaximenes proposes 'air' as the arche
v.s: Anaximenes of Miletus (c. 586–526 BCE), the last major representative of the Milesian school, argued that the arche of all things is 'air'. Through condensation and rarefaction, air transforms into wind, cloud, water, earth, and even stone. This theory retains the abstraction of his teacher Anaximander while returning to a concrete element, representing an important stage in the development of Greek natural philosophy.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Battle of Yanling: Jin defeats Chu, regains hegemony
v.s: Chu sent an army to relieve Zheng, which was under attack by Jin. Duke Li of Jin led his forces against King Gong of Chu at Yanling (present-day Yanling, Henan). The Jin army used a 'pincer movement' to shoot King Gong in the eye, throwing the Chu forces into disarray and forcing their night retreat. This great victory restored Jin's leadership over the Central Plains.

v.t: Construction of the Servian Wall begins in Rome
v.s: King Servius Tullius ordered the construction of a massive stone wall around the seven hills of Rome to defend against invaders. The wall was about 11 km long, with several gates and defensive towers, for the first time enclosing the entire Roman urban area. Known as the Servian Wall, it witnessed Rome's transformation from a hilltop settlement into a city-state.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Jin begins reclaiming ministerial fiefs to strengthen central authority
v.s: After the Battle of Yanling, Duke Li of Jin, seeing the growing power of ministerial families, began to take back some fiefs to reinforce central control. This policy aroused fierce resistance from old nobles like the Luan and Xi clans, intensifying domestic political struggles and ultimately leading to Duke Li's assassination.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Duke Li of Jin assassinated; Duke Dao ascends the throne
v.s: After Duke Li of Jin attempted to eliminate the powerful Luan and Xi families, Luan Shu and Zhonghang Yan jointly staged a coup, imprisoning and killing him. Subsequently, Luan Shu and others brought Sun Zhou, great-grandson of Duke Xiang of Jin, from Zhou to take the throne as Duke Dao of Jin. Only 14 years old, Duke Dao quickly purged the rebellious forces, showing remarkable political skill.

d.u: King Jian of Zhou, Ji Yi

v.t: Duke Dao of Jin implements new policies, reviving Jin's hegemony
v.s: After taking the throne, Duke Dao of Jin introduced a series of new policies to reverse the decline caused by internal strife: rebalancing the ministerial families and promoting capable non-hereditary officials; reducing taxes and allowing the people to rest; and reorganizing the army, strengthening the 'three armies and six ministers' system. These measures quickly revived Jin and regained the allegiance of vassal states, known as the 'Revival under Duke Dao of Jin'.

v.t: King Bimbisara of Magadha begins conquering neighboring states
v.s: Bimbisara (r. c. 558–491 BCE), king of the Indian state of Magadha, after consolidating Rajagriha (present-day Bihar, India), began expanding east and west, conquering neighboring states like Anga and establishing friendly relations with powerful states such as Kosala and Vajji through marriage alliances. His expansion laid a solid foundation for Magadha's eventual unification of northern India.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: King Ling of Zhou ascends throne; royal house further weakened
v.s: After the death of King Jian of Zhou, his son Ji Xixin ascended the throne as King Ling of Zhou. By this time, the domain directly ruled by the Zhou royal house had shrunk drastically, and its economic and military power was even less than that of a mid-sized vassal state. King Ling was still the nominal Son of Heaven, but vassals no longer paid regular tribute, and the king's authority was maintained only by limited rituals and kinship ties.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: City Dionysia festival formalized in Athens; Western drama born
v.s: The Athenian tyrant Peisistratus, to strengthen the cult of Dionysus and unify the populace, upgraded the rural Dionysia into a grand City Dionysia. In addition to processions and sacrifices, the festival included tragic competitions performed by chorus leaders. This event is considered the formal birth of Western drama, later producing giants like Aeschylus and Sophocles.

v.t: Laozi, founder of Daoism, writes and teaches in Chen or Ku County
v.s: Legend has it that Laozi (Li Er), having served for many years as keeper of the Zhou archives and gaining profound insight into Heaven and human affairs, resigned and went into seclusion. At the Hangu Pass, he was persuaded by Yin Xi, the keeper of the pass, to write a five-thousand-character work—the Tao Te Ching—advocating 'Dao follows nature' and 'governing by non-action', with profound influence on Chinese philosophy.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Lu enacts 'initial taxation of private fields', recognizing private land ownership
v.s: To increase revenue amid the struggle for hegemony, Duke Xuan of Lu ordered the 'initial taxation of private fields'—taxing both public and private fields according to their actual area. This effectively recognized private land ownership, breaking the well-field system tradition that 'land cannot be traded' since the Western Zhou. It was the first agricultural tax levied by land area in Chinese history, marking the emergence of feudal land ownership.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Greek colonists found Cyrene
v.s: Colonists from the Greek city-state Thera (modern Santorini) founded a new city on the Libyan coast of North Africa—Cyrene. The area had fertile soil and a pleasant climate, becoming an important source of grain and wool for the Greek world. Cyrene later became the most important Greek colony in North Africa and the birthplace of the mathematician Eratosthenes.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Lu astronomers systematically record solar eclipse and meteor shower
v.s: The scribes of Lu recorded in the 'Spring and Autumn Annals' a solar eclipse in the sixth month of that year and a meteor shower in winter, described as 'stars fell like rain'. These records are extremely valuable as evidence of systematic and sustained astronomical observation in the ancient world, providing essential data for later research.

v.t: Egyptian pharaoh Apries overthrown
v.s: Pharaoh Apries of the 26th Dynasty suffered a military mutiny after his failed campaign to aid Greek colonists at Cyrene. He was defeated and fled to his second capital, where he was captured and executed by the general Amasis (Ahmose II). Amasis declared himself pharaoh, beginning a period of relative stability and prosperity.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Panathenaic Games add athletic competitions
v.s: The Athenian tyrant Peisistratus, to celebrate the festival of Athena (the Panathenaea), added athletic competitions modeled on the Olympics, including horse racing, chariot racing, and the pentathlon. The prizes were amphorae of olive oil, a specialty of Athens, making the festival more attractive and cohesive.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Duke Dao of Jin convenes vassals at Jize, restoring Jin's prestige
v.s: To consolidate his hegemonic position, Duke Dao of Jin convened a meeting of nine vassal states (including Lu, Song, Wei, and Zheng) at Jize (present-day Handan, Hebei). He reformed the rituals, demanded that all vassals oppose Chu together, and sent ministers to strengthen military coordination in the east. This meeting marked the completion of Jin's hegemonic revival under Duke Dao.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Border conflict between Chu and Wu; Wu begins to rise
v.s: Wu, located east of Chu and instigated by Jin envoys, began to challenge Chu's authority. King Shoumeng of Wu attacked Chu border towns such as Chao and Xu, and the Chu chief commander Zifang counterattacked. Although Wu was repulsed, Jin's strategy of 'wearying Chu' by supporting Wu bore fruit, and Wu would become a perennial thorn in Chu's side.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Birth of Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha) at Lumbini
v.s: Siddhartha Gautama, crown prince of the Shakya kingdom of Kapilavastu (in present-day Nepal), was born at Lumbini. According to tradition, he took seven steps in each direction at birth and declared, 'I alone am the honored one in heaven and earth.' Later he would renounce palace life, practice austerities, and attain enlightenment under the Bodhi tree, founding Buddhism.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Death of Nebuchadnezzar II of Neo-Babylon; empire begins to decline
v.s: The great ancient monarch Nebuchadnezzar II died after a 43-year reign and was succeeded by his son Amel-Marduk. Although Nebuchadnezzar made Babylon the most prosperous city of the East, his death removed strong leadership, and internal class and religious conflicts intensified, leading the Neo-Babylonian Empire into decline.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Peisistratus establishes tyranny in Athens with popular support
v.s: The Athenian aristocrat Peisistratus seized power for the third time, this time with the backing of commoners and mercenaries, establishing a stable tyranny. He redistributed some aristocratic land to poor farmers, set up circuit courts in the countryside, vigorously developed commerce and maritime trade, and sponsored culture and the arts. Under his rule, Athens prospered economically and its cityscape was transformed.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Croesus becomes king of Lydia, introduces gold coinage
v.s: Croesus of Lydia (c. 595–546 BCE) ascended the throne. Using the kingdom's gold wealth, he reformed the currency, minting highly pure gold and silver coins known as 'Croeseids'. These reliable coins became hard currency for international trade, greatly promoting commercial activity in the eastern Mediterranean, and gave rise to the saying 'rich as Croesus'.

v.t: Duke Dao of Jin meets with seven states at Xiaoyu
v.s: To completely isolate Chu, Duke Dao of Jin met with seven states—Qi, Song, Lu, Wei, Cao, Ju, and Zhu—at Xiaoyu (present-day Xuchang, Henan), clarifying mutual military obligations against Chu. This meeting demonstrated Duke Dao's diplomatic skill and largely completed a strategic encirclement of Chu.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Cyrus II becomes leader of Persian tribes, begins to resist Media
v.s: Cyrus II (Cyrus the Great) of the Achaemenid clan succeeded his father as leader of the Persian tribes. Sensing the crisis within the Median kingdom, he secretly contacted Persian nobles unwilling to submit to Media and prepared for an armed uprising to overthrow Median rule.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Athenian tyrant Peisistratus issues first Athenian silver 'owl coins'
v.s: Peisistratus first became tyrant of Athens in 561/560 BCE. The famous Athenian 'owl coins' (Athena obverse, owl reverse) were first minted in large quantities around 525 BCE (under Peisistratus's son Hippias or after Cleisthenes' reforms). This entry is about 30 years too early.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Zhonghang Yan of Jin leads allied forces against Qin, reaching the Jing River
v.s: To retaliate for Qin's repeated support of Chu, Zhonghang Yan, the chief minister of Jin, led a 13-state coalition (including Qi, Song, and Lu) to attack Qin. The allies crossed the Jing River and reached Yulin (near present-day Jingyang, Shaanxi) deep in Qin territory, but due to lack of unity, they inflicted no fatal blow and withdrew after a symbolic victory.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Greek philosopher Xenophanes criticizes anthropomorphic gods
v.s: The Greek rhapsode and philosopher Xenophanes (c. 570–475 BCE) argued that the gods are not like the anthropomorphic figures of Homeric epic. He pointedly noted that if oxen, horses, or lions had hands and could paint, they would paint gods in their own image. This critique opened the way for rational theology in later Greek philosophy.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Croesus of Lydia conquers Greek cities in Asia Minor
v.s: Using his great wealth, King Croesus of Lydia expanded eastward, conquering Greek colonies on the western coast of Asia Minor, including Ephesus and Miletus. These cities were forced to pay tribute and submit to Lydia, making Croesus the hegemon of western Asia Minor.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Cyrus II secretly contacts Persian tribes, prepares revolt
v.s: After years of preparation, Cyrus II had united major Persian tribes such as Pasargadae and Margiana and established secret contact with Harpagus, a Median noble discontented with King Astyages. The revolt against Media was imminent.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Cyrus II openly rebels against Media, launching Persian War of Independence
v.s: Cyrus II gathered all Persian tribal leaders at Pasargadae and formally declared independence from Media. He appointed his son as a general and attacked Median garrisons. King Astyages of Media led a large army to suppress the revolt, and the two forces engaged in battle, marking the start of the Persian War of Independence.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Persian army defeats Media; Astyages captured
v.s: With Harpagus's betrayal, Cyrus II led the Persian army to a decisive victory over the Medes, capturing King Astyages. Cyrus treated his captive well, then occupied the Median capital Ecbatana and annexed Media, making himself the most powerful monarch in western Asia.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Birth of Confucius, founder of Confucianism
v.s: The great ancient Chinese thinker and educator Confucius (Kong Qiu, courtesy name Zhongni) was born in Zouyi (present-day Qufu, Shandong) in the state of Lu. He lost his father early and grew up poor but was diligent in learning, mastering the 'six arts'. He later founded the Confucian school, with 'benevolence' and 'ritual propriety' as core concepts, and pioneered private education, exerting the most profound influence on Chinese and East Asian civilization.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Cyrus II establishes capital at Pasargadae, founding the Persian Empire
v.s: After conquering Media, Cyrus II formally established his new capital at Pasargadae (in present-day Fars Province, Iran), marking the official birth of the Achaemenid Persian Empire. He then reformed the army, mixing Median and Persian warriors into a standing force in preparation for future expeditions.

v.t: Hecataeus of Miletus draws a world map
v.s: The Greek historian and geographer Hecataeus (c. 550–476 BCE) drew a more accurate 'world map' based on that of Anaximander. It depicted the world as a circular continent with the Mediterranean at its center, surrounded by ocean, and described the geography of Europe, Asia, and Africa.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Cyrus II conquers Parthia and Hyrcania
v.s: Cyrus II marched east, conquering Parthia and Hyrcania on the southeastern shore of the Caspian Sea, adding these lands to the Persian Empire. This victory opened the door for further eastern expansion and provided the empire with strategic depth and a source of fine horses.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Death of Anaximenes, the Milesian philosopher
v.s: Anaximenes of Miletus died. He was the last major representative of the Milesian school. His theory that 'air' is the arche and his explanation of condensation and rarefaction enriched early natural philosophy and influenced later thinkers such as Heraclitus.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Cyrus II conquers Lydia, captures King Croesus
v.s: To contain Persian expansion, King Croesus of Lydia marched east. The two armies fought an indecisive battle at Pteria (in present-day Turkey). Cyrus then pursued Croesus into Lydia and defeated the Lydian army near Sardis, capturing the city and Croesus. The wealthy Lydian kingdom was destroyed.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Persia conquers Greek city-states in Asia Minor, sparking cultural contact
v.s: Cyrus II sent his general Mazares to conquer the Greek city-states of the western Asia Minor coast, including Miletus and Ephesus. These cities were forced to pay tribute and accept Persian governors. This was the first time Greek civilization was ruled by an Eastern autocratic empire, but the contact also fostered intellectual and cultural exchange.

v.t: Second Disarmament Conference: Jin and Chu share hegemony, ushering in peace
v.s: Through the mediation of Xiang Xu, a minister of Song, representatives of fourteen states (including Jin, Chu, Qi, and Qin) held the second disarmament conference at Song's capital Shangqiu. The agreement called for cessation of hostilities, with Jin and Chu sharing the role of hegemon; other states would pay tribute to both. This led to over forty years of peace in the Central Plains, allowing internal socio-economic development.

d.u: King Ling of Zhou, Ji Xixin

v.t: Death of King Ling of Zhou; King Jing ascends throne
v.s: King Ling of Zhou (Ji Xixin) died of illness and was succeeded by his son Ji Gui as King Jing of Zhou. By this time, the Zhou royal treasury was exhausted, even requiring loans from vassals to pay for the funeral. The king's authority had hit rock bottom, maintained only as an empty name as nominal Son of Heaven.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: King Yu Ji of Wu assassinated by a Yue captive; Wu-Yue enmity deepens
v.s: King Yu Ji of Wu was stabbed to death by a captive from Yue while inspecting his fleet. His brother Yu Mei succeeded him. The long-standing hostility between Wu and Yue deepened further, setting the stage for the all-out wars between King Helü and King Fuchai of Wu and King Goujian of Yue.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Zichan takes power in Zheng, initiates reforms
v.s: The famous Spring and Autumn statesman Zichan (Gongsun Qiao) became chief minister of Zheng. Sandwiched between the great powers Jin and Chu, he carried out internal reforms to create clear hierarchies between capital and countryside, while flexibly maintaining Zheng's independence without challenging the hegemons. His governance kept Zheng stable and prosperous.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Ostracism introduced in Athens
v.s: Before Cleisthenes' reforms, Athens may have begun experimenting with ostracism. Under this system, the popular assembly could vote once a year: if any person received more than 6,000 votes as a 'threat to democracy', he would be exiled for ten years. The aim was to prevent would-be tyrants from seizing power, and it became an important safeguard of Athenian democracy.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Cyrus II conquers Central Asia, reaches the Jaxartes
v.s: Cyrus II led an eastern expedition, conquering Bactria (northern Afghanistan), Sogdiana (Uzbekistan), and other Central Asian regions, advancing to the Jaxartes (Syr Darya), which became the easternmost border of the Persian Empire. These regions became important sources of horses and grain.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Qin widely adopts the district (xian) system in newly conquered areas
v.s: During the reign of Duke Jing of Qin, the areas conquered from the Rong people and places like Gui and Ji were converted into districts administered directly by officials appointed by the duke, with no interference from hereditary nobles. This broke with the traditional enfeoffment system, strengthened central control, and laid the foundation for later nationwide adoption of the district system under Shang Yang.

v.t: Sparta establishes the Peloponnesian League, dominating southern Greece
v.s: Through a series of wars and diplomatic maneuvers, Sparta defeated Argos and forced Corinth, Sicyon, Epidaurus, and other Peloponnesian cities to join a military alliance led by Sparta—the Peloponnesian League. With political systems aligned to Sparta, the league became the bulwark of Spartan power against the expansion of Athenian democracy.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Cyrus the Great conquers Babylon; last king Belshazzar killed
v.s: Cyrus II marched against the Neo-Babylonian Empire. He cleverly used the seasonal low water of the Euphrates to divert the river and march his troops into the city along the dry riverbed. Babylon fell without a fight, and King Belshazzar was killed in the chaos. Cyrus adopted a conciliatory policy, respecting local religion and customs, which helped consolidate his rule.

v.t: Cyrus issues edict allowing Jews to return to their homeland
v.s: After conquering Babylon, Cyrus the Great issued the famous 'Edict of Cyrus', allowing the exiled Jews of Babylon to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple. He also returned the sacred vessels that Nebuchadnezzar had plundered from Jerusalem. This marked the end of the 'Babylonian Captivity' and was of great significance for the Jewish people and Judaism.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: First group of Jews departs for Jerusalem
v.s: In response to the Edict of Cyrus, the first group of about 50,000 Jews, led by Sheshbazzar and Zerubbabel, left Babylon for Jerusalem. Carrying gifts and Temple vessels provided by Cyrus, they began the arduous journey of rebuilding their homeland and the Temple.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Persia conquers the Indus Valley, reaching its greatest territorial extent
v.s: Cyrus II sent a general to conquer the Gandhara region of the northwestern Indus plain, making it a province of the Persian Empire. The empire now spanned three continents—Asia, Africa, and Europe—stretching from the Indus to the Aegean, and from the Caucasus to the Nile, becoming the largest empire in the world at that time.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Zichan of Zheng 'casts the penal code', publishing written law
v.s: Zichan, the chief minister of Zheng, had the state's laws cast onto a bronze tripod, making them public. This broke with the tradition that 'if laws are unknown, the ruler's power is unfathomable', allowing the people to know the law. Although criticized by conservatives like Shu Xiang of Jin, it was the first formal publication of written law in Chinese history and a landmark event.

v.t: Birth of the Greek philosopher Heraclitus
v.s: Heraclitus of Ephesus (c. 535–475 BCE) was born into an aristocratic family. He proposed that the arche is 'fire' and that the universe is in 'constant flux'. His famous saying 'No man ever steps in the same river twice' profoundly influenced later dialectical thought.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Thespis plays a role in Athens, hailed as the 'first actor'
v.s: At the City Dionysia in Athens, the poet Thespis separated himself from the traditional chorus leader for the first time, engaging in dialogue and performance with the chorus. This marked the birth of the 'actor' in Western drama, and Thespis has been called the first actor, with his name becoming a synonym for performer.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: King Servius Tullius murdered by his daughter; Tarquinius seizes throne
v.s: Servius Tullius was assassinated in a coup led by his son-in-law Lucius Tarquinius (Tarquin the Proud), who then declared himself the seventh king of Rome. Tarquinius was an Etruscan whose brutal and arbitrary rule aroused widespread discontent among the aristocracy, sowing the seeds for the end of the Roman monarchy.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Persian Empire builds an extensive courier and Royal Road system
v.s: Cyrus the Great and his successors began building royal roads linking the empire's major cities. The most famous, from Susa through Sardis to Ephesus, was about 2,500 km long, with 111 stations equipped with messengers and fast horses. This efficient communications system, known as the 'Persian Royal Road', greatly facilitated the transmission of orders and commerce.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Siddhartha Gautama renounces worldly life in search of liberation
v.s: At age 29, Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha), witnessing the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death, renounced his royal life, leaving his wife and newborn son to become a wandering ascetic. He studied under two meditation masters and then practiced extreme austerity for six years, but finding no ultimate liberation, he chose the Middle Path.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Confucius begins work as a 'ru' (ritual master), studying rites and music
v.s: In his youth, because his family was poor, Confucius began working as a 'ru' (performing funerals, sacrifices, and other ceremonial duties for the nobility). He seized every opportunity to learn the 'six arts' (rites, music, archery, charioteering, calligraphy, mathematics), showing particular interest in the ritual system, which laid a solid foundation for his later achievements as a great scholar.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Cyrus the Great dies in battle in Central Asia; Cambyses II succeeds
v.s: Cyrus II, founder of the Persian Empire, was killed while campaigning against the Massagetae, a Central Asian nomadic tribe led by Queen Tomyris. His body was found by the Massagetae, and Tomyris put his head in a wineskin filled with blood. His eldest son, Cambyses II, inherited the throne.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Cambyses II consolidates power and prepares to invade Egypt
v.s: After coming to power, Cambyses II secretly executed his own brother Bardiya, who might have threatened his throne, then quickly put down some revolts in the east. Following his father's expansionist policy, he began military and logistical preparations for a full-scale invasion of Egypt, including allying with Arab tribes to secure water supplies across the Sinai.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Siddhartha attains enlightenment under the Bodhi tree
v.s: After abandoning asceticism, Siddhartha accepted a bowl of rice pudding from a milkmaid, regained his strength, and sat in meditation under a peepal tree (later the Bodhi tree), vowing not to rise until he had attained enlightenment. After forty-nine days, he finally understood the truth of the universe and became the Buddha ('the awakened one').

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Death of Peisistratus; his sons succeed as tyrants of Athens
v.s: Peisistratus died peacefully in Athens and was succeeded by his sons Hippias and Hipparchus as co-tyrants. His rule had brought Athens prosperity and stability, but after his death, the aristocratic democratic faction revived, and the tyranny became increasingly oppressive, eventually leading to its downfall.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Death of Pharaoh Ahmose II of Egypt; Psamtik III succeeds
v.s: Ahmose II, the last strong pharaoh of Egypt's 26th Dynasty, died and was succeeded by his son Psamtik III. By this time, the Persian army was already massing on Egypt's border. The new pharaoh prepared a hasty resistance, but could not withstand Persian power, and Egypt's independence was about to end.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Cambyses II conquers Egypt, establishing the 27th Dynasty
v.s: Cambyses II led a Persian army across the Sinai with the help of Arab tribes, defeated the Egyptian forces at the Battle of Pelusium, captured Memphis, and took Pharaoh Psamtik III prisoner. Cambyses assumed the title of pharaoh, and Egypt became a province of the Persian Empire, beginning the 27th Dynasty (First Persian Period).

v.t: Aeschylus begins writing tragedies in Athens
v.s: The great Greek tragic poet Aeschylus (c. 525–456 BCE) was born in this year. He is later known as the 'father of tragedy', wrote about 70–90 plays, increased the number of actors from one to two, and introduced stage sets and props, greatly enhancing dramatic expression.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Chu reforms its military, creates 'Left and Right Guang' royal guard
v.s: To strengthen royal power, King Kang of Chu reformed the military by selecting elite from the noble youth to form two chariot units directly under the king, called the 'Left Guang' and 'Right Guang', each with 30 chariots. This was the first known standing royal army in Chinese history, greatly enhancing the king's military command and domestic deterrence.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Buddha delivers his first sermon at Deer Park, founding the sangha
v.s: After his enlightenment, the Buddha went to the Deer Park at Sarnath and found the five ascetics who had previously accompanied him. He taught them the 'Four Noble Truths' and the 'Eightfold Path', and they became the first bhikkhus (monks), marking the founding of the Buddhist sangha. Buddhism began to spread as an independent religious force in ancient India.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Civil war in the Persian Empire; Darius I kills false king and ascends throne
v.s: Cambyses II died while returning from Egypt. A priest named Gaumata, impersonating Bardiya (the prince Cambyses had secretly executed), started rebellions across the empire. Darius I, together with six nobles, killed Gaumata, suppressed the rebellions, and took the throne. Darius was from a collateral branch of the Achaemenid family, and his legitimacy was challenged.

v.t: Wu Zixu flees to Wu after his father and brother are killed by King Ping of Chu
v.s: The Chu grandee Wu She and his eldest son Wu Shang were executed on false charges by King Ping of Chu. Wu She's second son, Wu Zixu (Wu Yuan), was forced to flee and, after many hardships, reached the state of Wu. He vowed to avenge his father and brother and later became a key minister of King Helü of Wu, playing a crucial role in Wu's rise and eventual attack on Chu.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gui

v.t: Darius I suppresses revolts in Babylon, Elam, and elsewhere
v.s: Newly crowned Darius I faced multiple revolts in Babylon, Elam, Media, and other regions. Through more than a year of brutal warfare, he suppressed them one by one. In Babylon, he ordered the city walls demolished and executed many rebels, re-establishing Persian rule over Mesopotamia.

d.u: King Dao of Zhou, Ji Meng

v.t: Death of King Jing of Zhou; revolt of Prince Chao
v.s: King Jing of Zhou (Ji Gui) died of illness. Before his death, he favored his eldest son by a concubine, Prince Chao, wanting to make him crown prince, but was opposed by ministers. After the king's death, the ministers installed Crown Prince Ji Meng as King Dao of Zhou. Prince Chao, supported by some nobles, raised an army and attacked the king, sparking a large-scale civil war that lasted several years.

v.t: Darius I orders the Behistun Inscription
v.s: To proclaim his accomplishments in suppressing revolts and legitimize his rule, Darius I ordered a long inscription carved in three languages (Old Persian, Elamite, Akkadian) on a cliff at Behistun in Kermanshah. Above the inscription, a relief shows Darius trampling the false king Gaumata and facing bound rebel leaders. The inscription became key to deciphering cuneiform.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: King Dao of Zhou killed; King Jing installed; Prince Chao continues revolt
v.s: King Dao of Zhou (Ji Meng) died of fright during Prince Chao's attack only months into his reign. Jin sent troops to escort Dao's younger brother Ji Gai to Luoyi, where he was enthroned as King Jing of Zhou. Prince Chao held the Wangcheng (west of Luoyang) and called himself king, resulting in two kings reigning simultaneously, and the civil war continued.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Darius I reforms Persian administration, creates twenty satrapies
v.s: After suppressing the revolts, Darius I carried out comprehensive administrative reforms: the empire was divided into about twenty satrapies (provinces), each governed by a satrap directly appointed by the king; uniform coinage, tax, and measurement systems were introduced; and a system of 'the king's eyes and ears' (inspectors not bound by local authority) was established to supervise the satraps. The reforms strengthened central control.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Foundation of the Second Temple in Jerusalem
v.s: Encouraged by the prophets Haggai and Zechariah, Zerubbabel and the high priest Joshua led the returned Jewish exiles in a foundation ceremony for the Second Temple on the site of the original Temple in Jerusalem. Though reconstruction was later stalled by opposition from neighboring peoples like the Samaritans, a crucial first step had been taken.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Jin-led forces assist King Jing of Zhou; Prince Chao flees to Chu
v.s: King Jing of Zhou requested military aid from Jin. Duke Qing of Jin sent generals Ji Tan and Xun Lu with allied forces to attack Prince Chao. Prince Chao was defeated and fled to Chu, taking with him many Zhou classical texts and ritual vessels. King Jing finally entered the Wangcheng, ending the several-year revolt, but the Zhou royal house was severely weakened.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Zhuan Zhu assassinates King Liao of Wu; Prince Guang seizes throne as King Helü
v.s: To take the throne, Prince Guang of Wu (later King Helü) invited King Liao to a banquet and had the assassin Zhuan Zhu conceal a dagger inside a fish. Zhuan Zhu killed King Liao but was himself killed by the guards. Prince Guang then took the throne as King Helü of Wu. He empowered Wu Zixu and Sun Wu, and Wu began to grow strong.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Darius I campaigns against the Scythians; fails to conquer but secures frontiers
v.s: Darius I led his army across the Bosporus to campaign against the Scythians (Scythians) north of the Black Sea. The Scythians used scorched-earth tactics, luring the Persians deep into the steppe. Unable to force a decisive battle, Darius withdrew with heavy losses. Nevertheless, the Persian army established footholds in Thrace and along the Black Sea coast.

v.t: The Fan and Zhonghang clans of Jin's Six Ministers expel the Zhao clan
v.s: Conflicts among the six ministerial families of Jin (Zhao, Han, Wei, Zhi, Fan, Zhonghang) escalated. The Fan and Zhonghang clans attacked the Zhao, and Zhao Jianzi (Zhao Yang) was forced to flee to Jinyang (present-day Taiyuan, Shanxi). Civil war broke out in Jin, shattering the balance among the Six Ministers.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Zhao Yang, allied with Zhi, Han, and Wei, defeats Fan and Zhonghang
v.s: After holding out for a year at Jinyang, Zhao Jianzi allied with the Zhi, Han, and Wei clans and petitioned Duke Ding of Jin to order a campaign against the Fan and Zhonghang. The allied forces defeated and expelled the Fan and Zhonghang clans, after which the four clans of Zhao, Han, Wei, and Zhi controlled Jin's government, and the territories of Fan and Zhonghang were partitioned.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Persia conquers Thrace; Macedon becomes a vassal
v.s: In the aftermath of Darius I's Scythian campaign, the Persian army conquered Thrace (northern Greece and southern Bulgaria) and forced King Alexander I of Macedon to pay tribute and submit as a vassal. This was the first time Persian power reached the Balkan Peninsula of Europe.

v.t: Sun Wu presents 'The Art of War' to King Helü of Wu
v.s: Sun Wu (c. 545–470 BCE), a man from Qi, was recommended by Wu Zixu seven times and presented his thirteen-chapter treatise on military strategy to King Helü of Wu. The king was deeply impressed, and after testing Sun Wu's ability by training palace women, he appointed him general. Sun Wu's work became the foundation of ancient Chinese military theory, hailed as the 'sacred classic of war'.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: King Helü of Wu adopts Wu Zixu's 'exhaust Chu' strategy
v.s: Wu Zixu advised King Helü to divide the Wu army into three groups that would rotate raiding Chu's border. Whenever Chu's main army moved to engage, the Wu force would withdraw, wearing out the Chu forces. The king adopted the plan, and after years of harassment, Chu's border region was devastated and its national power greatly reduced.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Rome expels King Tarquinius; end of the Roman monarchy
v.s: Because Sextus, son of King Lucius Tarquinius (Tarquin the Proud), raped Lucretia, the wife of the noble Lucius Tarquinius Collatinus, Lucretia committed suicide in shame. Lucius, along with Brutus and other nobles, led an uprising that expelled Tarquinius and his family. The Senate decided not to appoint a new king, ending the Roman monarchical period.

v.t: Pythagoras establishes a school in Croton, teaching mathematics and philosophy
v.s: To escape the tyranny of Polycrates, Pythagoras moved from Samos to Croton in southern Italy. There he founded a secret society that was part religious, part political, and part academic—the Pythagorean school—teaching mathematics, music, and philosophy, with strict ascetic rules for its followers.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: The Roman Republic is established; first consuls take office
v.s: After overthrowing the monarchy, the Roman aristocrats established a republic and elected Lucius Junius Brutus and Lucius Tarquinius Collatinus as the first consuls. The consuls' power was limited by the Senate and the popular assemblies. The Roman republican system would profoundly influence Western political systems.

v.t: Confucius begins to take disciples and teach, pioneering private education
v.s: Around this time, Confucius began to teach disciples, instructing them in the 'six arts': rites, music, archery, charioteering, calligraphy, and mathematics. He advocated 'education without class distinction', allowing anyone regardless of wealth or status to study, breaking the monopoly of 'official education'. His disciples came from various states, with seventy-two notable ones, forming the foundation of the Confucian school.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Cleisthenes' democratic reforms in Athens: creation of ten tribes
v.s: After overthrowing the tyrant Hippias, the Athenian aristocrat Cleisthenes carried out radical political reforms. He replaced the traditional four blood-tribes with ten territorial tribes, established a Council of Five Hundred, and introduced ostracism. These reforms broke the aristocratic monopoly on power and marked the establishment of Athenian democracy.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Roman Republic signs its first treaty with Carthage
v.s: The newly founded Roman Republic signed a treaty with the North African power Carthage, delineating their commercial spheres. Roman merchants were not to sail south of Carthaginian-controlled North Africa and Sardinia, while Carthage recognized Roman leadership in Latium. This was the first recorded external treaty in Roman history.

v.t: Yan Ying of Qi visits Jin, discusses politics with Shu Xiang
v.s: The Qi minister Yan Ying went on a mission to Jin and had a private conversation with the Jin minister Shu Xiang. Yan Ying predicted that Qi's power would fall into the hands of the Tian (Chen) clan, while Shu Xiang lamented that Jin would be taken over by cadet branches of the ducal house. Their conversation accurately foresaw the later 'Tian replacing Qi' and 'Partition of Jin into Three'.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Wu enters Ying, Wu Zixu exhumed the corpse of King Ping of Chu
v.s: King Helü of Wu, together with Wu Zixu and Sun Wu, leading a Wu army in coordination with Tang and Cai, launched a decisive attack on Chu. Avoiding Chu's main defenses, the Wu army made a flanking march up the Han River, winning five battles in a row and capturing the Chu capital, Ying. Wu Zixu found King Ping's tomb, exhumed and flogged his corpse to avenge his father and brother. Chu was nearly destroyed.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Shen Baoxu weeps at the Qin court for seven days, Qin agrees to help Chu restore its kingdom
v.s: The Chu grandee Shen Baoxu went to Qin to request aid. Duke Ai of Qin was initially unwilling. Shen Baoxu wept at the palace gate for seven days and nights without eating or drinking. Moved by his loyalty, Duke Ai dispatched 500 war chariots to rescue Chu. The Qin army defeated Wu at Ji, and King Zhao of Chu was able to return to Ying and restore the state.

v.t: Death of the ancient Greek philosopher Pythagoras
v.s: Pythagoras died in Croton. His school made great achievements in mathematics, astronomy, and music theory, discovering the golden ratio and proving the Pythagorean theorem. His ideas of the transmigration of souls and 'all is number' deeply influenced later Platonism and Neoplatonism.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Roman consul Valerius passes the 'Law on Appeal'
v.s: The Roman consul Publius Valerius Publicola, to further the transformation from monarchy to republic, passed a law granting any citizen the right to appeal a magistrate's decision to the popular assembly. This law limited the capital power of magistrates and protected the basic rights of citizens.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: King Yun Chang of Yue proclaims himself king, escalating conflict with Wu
v.s: During the reign of King Yun Chang of Yue, Yue grew in power. He formally declared himself king, openly challenging King Helü of Wu. The two states engaged in frequent border clashes, beginning the era of major conflict between Wu and Yue.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Darius I builds the Royal Road from Susa to Sardis
v.s: To strengthen communication between the eastern and western satrapies, Darius I ordered the construction and improvement of the 'Royal Road' from the capital Susa to Sardis in Asia Minor. The road was about 2,500 km long, with stations and guards at intervals, enabling messengers to cover the entire distance in seven days. It was the most advanced transportation and communications system in the world at the time.

d.u: King Jing of Zhou, Ji Gai

v.t: Confucius appointed magistrate of Zhongdu in Lu, achieves notable results
v.s: Thanks to his profound learning and good reputation, Confucius was appointed magistrate of Zhongdu (present-day Wenshang, Shandong) by Duke Ding of Lu. In office, he promoted ritual propriety, educated the people, and implemented measures such as giving the young and old different food and separating the sexes on the roads, bringing order and prosperity to Zhongdu. This was his first administrative post.

v.t: Rome creates the dictatorship to handle wartime emergencies
v.s: Facing frequent incursions by neighboring tribes, the Senate and popular assembly created the office of 'dictator'. In times of crisis, a consul could nominate a dictator who would hold supreme power beyond all laws for up to six months, enabling focused military command. The first dictator, Titus Larcius, was appointed to deal with threats from the Latin League.

v.t: Greek tragic poet Phrynichus wins first prize in Athens
v.s: Phrynichus (c. 540–470 BCE), a predecessor of Aeschylus, won his first victory in the tragic competition at the City Dionysia in this year with his play 'The Fall of Miletus'. The play dealt with a real historical event—the Persian conquest of Miletus—and was so moving that the poet was fined, but it pioneered the use of contemporary history as tragic subject matter.
